title: Somatic in the East, Psychological in the West?: Investigating Clinically-Grounded Cross-Cultural Depression Symptom Expression in LLMs
abstract: arXiv:2508.03247v2 Announce Type: replace  Abstract: Prior clinical psychology research shows that Western individuals with depression tend to report psychological symptoms, while Eastern individuals report somatic ones. We test whether Large Language Models (LLMs), which are increasingly used in mental health, reproduce these cultural patterns by prompting them with Western or Eastern personas. Results show that LLMs largely fail to replicate the patterns when prompted in English, though prompting in major Eastern languages (i.e., Chinese, Japanese, and Hindi) improves alignment in several configurations. Our analysis pinpoints two key reasons for this failure: the models' low sensitivity to cultural personas and a strong, culturally invariant symptom hierarchy that overrides cultural cues. These findings reveal that while prompt language is important, current general-purpose LLMs lack the robust, culture-aware capabilities essential for safe and effective mental health applications.

Title: Somatic in the East, Psychological in the West?: Investigating Clinically-Grounded Cross-Cultural Depression Symptom Expression in LLMs
Abstract: arXiv:2508.03247v2 Announce Type: replace  Abstract: Prior clinical psychology research shows that Western individuals with depression tend to report psychological symptoms, while Eastern individuals report somatic ones. We test whether Large Language Models (LLMs), which are increasingly used in mental health, reproduce these cultural patterns by prompting them with Western or Eastern personas. Results show that LLMs largely fail to replicate the patterns when prompted in English, though prompting in major Eastern languages (i.e., Chinese, Japanese, and Hindi) improves alignment in several configurations. Our analysis pinpoints two key reasons for this failure: the models' low sensitivity to cultural personas and a strong, culturally invariant symptom hierarchy that overrides cultural cues. These findings reveal that while prompt language is important, current general-purpose LLMs lack the robust, culture-aware capabilities essential for safe and effective mental health applications.
translated_title: 躯体化在东方，心理化在西方？探究基于临床证据的跨文化抑郁症状表达在大语言模型中的表现
translated_abstract: 摘要：先前的临床心理学研究表明，西方抑郁症患者倾向于报告心理症状，而东方患者则更多报告躯体症状。我们测试了在心理健康领域应用日益广泛的大语言模型（LLMs）是否会通过赋予其西方或东方角色来重现这些文化模式。结果表明，当使用英文提示时，LLMs在很大程度上未能复现这些模式，但使用主要东方语言（即中文、日语和印地语）进行提示时，在若干配置下一致性有所提升。我们的分析指出了这一失败的两个关键原因：模型对文化角色的敏感性较低，以及存在一种强烈的、文化不变的症状层级结构，这种结构会覆盖文化线索。这些发现表明，尽管提示语言很重要，但当前通用大语言模型缺乏稳健的、具备文化意识的必要能力，无法确保安全有效的心理健康应用。
tldr: 本文发现大语言模型在跨文化抑郁症状表达上存在显著缺陷，主要原因是模型对文化角色敏感性低且存在文化不变的症状层级结构，这揭示了当前通用LLMs缺乏稳健的文化意识能力。
en_tdlr: This paper reveals that large language models exhibit significant deficiencies in cross-cultural depression symptom expression, primarily due to low sensitivity to cultural personas and a culturally invariant symptom hierarchy, highlighting the lack of robust culture-aware capabilities in current general-purpose LLMs.
